sections: The Crown and the Australian Defence Force
first_paragraph: The **monarchy of Australia** is a central component of [Australia's system of government](/wiki/Politics_of_Australia "Politics of Australia"), by which a [hereditary monarch](/wiki/Hereditary_monarch "Hereditary monarch") serves as the country's [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereign "Sovereign") and [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), modelled on the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") of [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy") and [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government"), while incorporating features unique to the [Constitution of Australia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia").

The **monarchy of Australia** is a central component of [Australia's system of government](/wiki/Politics_of_Australia "Politics of Australia"), by which a [hereditary monarch](/wiki/Hereditary_monarch "Hereditary monarch") serves as the country's [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereign "Sovereign") and [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), modelled on the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") of [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy") and [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government"), while incorporating features unique to the [Constitution of Australia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia").

The present monarch is [King Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), who has reigned since 8 September 2022.[[a]](#cite_note-accession-4) The monarch is represented at the federal level by the [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-general_of_Australia "Governor-general of Australia") (currently [Samantha Mostyn](/wiki/Samantha_Mostyn "Samantha Mostyn")[[4]](#cite_note-5)), in accordance with the Australian Constitution[[5]](#cite_note-sovereignty-6) and [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") from his mother and predecessor, Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II").[[6]](#cite_note-7) Similarly, in each of the [Australian states](/wiki/Australian_states "Australian states") the monarch is represented by a [governor](/wiki/Governors_of_the_Australian_states "Governors of the Australian states") (assisted by a lieutenant-governor; generally the chief justice of the state's supreme court), according to the *[Australia Act](/wiki/Australia_Act_1986 "Australia Act 1986")* and respective letters patent and state constitutions.[[7]](#cite_note-:2-8) In the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory") (which is legally subordinate to the federal government), the monarch is represented by an [administrator](/wiki/Administrator_of_the_Northern_Territory "Administrator of the Northern Territory") appointed by the governor-general.[[8]](#cite_note-9) The monarch appoints the governor-general on the [advice](/wiki/Advice_(constitutional_law) "Advice (constitutional law)") of the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Australia "Prime Minister of Australia"), and appoints the state governors on the advice of the respective [premiers](/wiki/Premiers_of_Australia "Premiers of Australia").[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-:1-11) These are the only mandatory constitutional functions of the monarch of Australia.[[11]](#cite_note-:5-12)

[Australian constitutional law](/wiki/Australian_constitutional_law "Australian constitutional law") provides that the person who is [monarch of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Monarch_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarch of the United Kingdom") will also be the monarch of Australia.[[5]](#cite_note-sovereignty-6)[[12]](#cite_note-13) Since the 1940s at the latest, the Australian monarchy has been a distinct office in which the monarch acts in accordance with the advice of an Australian prime minister or state premier in relevant context. Australia is one of the [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm"), 15 independent countries that share the same [person as monarch and head of state](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union").[[13]](#cite_note-:4-14)

## International and domestic aspects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: International and domestic aspects")]

Further information: [Commonwealth realm § Relationship of the realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm#Relationship_of_the_realms "Commonwealth realm")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/2024_Royal_Visit_Sydney_%281%29.jpg/250px-2024_Royal_Visit_Sydney_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2024_Royal_Visit_Sydney_(1).jpg)

Sydney Opera House lit up with images of previous royal tours of Australia by [Charles](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") and [Camilla](/wiki/Queen_Camilla "Queen Camilla"), 2024

The monarch of Australia is the same person as the monarch of the 14 other [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") within the 56-member [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations").[[13]](#cite_note-:4-14) However, each realm is independent of the others, the monarchy in each being distinct from the rest.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-Sue_v_Hill-16) Effective with the *[Australia Act 1986](/wiki/Australia_Act_1986 "Australia Act 1986"),* the British government cannot advise the monarch on any matters pertinent to Australia; on all matters of the Australian Commonwealth, the monarch is advised solely by Australian federal [ministers of state](/wiki/Minister_of_the_Crown "Minister of the Crown").[[16]](#cite_note-RAC-17) Likewise, on all matters relating to any Australian state, the monarch is advised by the ministers of that state, tendered via the premier.[[7]](#cite_note-:2-8)

### Emergence of a separate Crown

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Emergence of a separate Crown")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/2024_Royal_Visit_Sydney_%284%29.jpg/250px-2024_Royal_Visit_Sydney_%284%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2024_Royal_Visit_Sydney_(4).jpg)

King Charles III and Queen Camilla in front of the [Sydney Harbour Bridge](/wiki/Sydney_Harbour_Bridge "Sydney Harbour Bridge"), 2024

Courts and academics have proposed several dates on which the Crown of Australia separated from the [Crown of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Crown_of_the_United_Kingdom "Crown of the United Kingdom").[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETwomey2010457–461-18) These include 1926, when at [an Imperial Conference](/wiki/1926_Imperial_Conference "1926 Imperial Conference") it was announced that governors-general would no longer represent the government of the United Kingdom or 1930, when at [another Imperial Conference](/wiki/1930_Imperial_Conference "1930 Imperial Conference") it was clarified that the monarch would be advised directly by dominion ministers. [Anne Twomey](/wiki/Anne_Twomey_(academic) "Anne Twomey (academic)") argues for this later date at the latest.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETwomey2010459-19) Others have suggested the Crowns separated once Australia became fully independent, with dates suggested including 1931 (when the UK *[Statute of Westminster](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931")* was passed), 1939 or 1942 (due to the *[Statute of Westminster Adoption Act](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_Adoption_Act_1942 "Statute of Westminster Adoption Act 1942")* (Cth), passed in 1942 with effect retrospectively to 1939) or 1986 (when the *[Australia Acts](/wiki/Australia_Acts "Australia Acts")* severed the last possibilities of UK institutions changing Australian laws).[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETwomey2010459–461-20) However, members of the High Court have indicated that the separation of the Crowns was complete by at least 1948, as seen by the creation of [Australian citizenship laws](/wiki/Australian_citizenship_law "Australian citizenship law").[[20]](#cite_note-21)

Despite the emergence of an Australian Crown at the federal level, prior to the *Australia Acts* in 1986 the states still operated under the Crown of the United Kingdom. It has been debated whether the end of that relationship in 1986 led to a unified Australian crown or created, in addition to the federal crown, separate crowns for each state.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETwomey2010456–479-22) However, this distinction is of little practical importance while the states and the Commonwealth continue to recognise the same sovereign.

### Title

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Title")]

Further information: [List of titles and honours of Charles III](/wiki/List_of_titles_and_honours_of_Charles_III "List of titles and honours of Charles III") and [Commonwealth realm § Titles](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm#Titles "Commonwealth realm")

The formal title of the current monarch is *King*[[b]](#cite_note-KingTitle-24) *Charles the Third, [by the Grace of God](/wiki/By_the_Grace_of_God "By the Grace of God") King of Australia and His other Realms and Territories, [Head of the Commonwealth](/wiki/Head_of_the_Commonwealth "Head of the Commonwealth")*.[[23]](#cite_note-25)[[24]](#cite_note-pmc-title1-26)[[25]](#cite_note-pmc-title2-27)[[c]](#cite_note-36)

Prior to 1953, the title of the Australian monarch had simply been the same as [that in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Style_of_the_British_sovereign "Style of the British sovereign"). A change in the title resulted from occasional discussion among Commonwealth prime ministers and an eventual meeting in London in December 1952, at which Australia's officials stated their preference for a format for Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II")'s title that would name all the realms. However, they stated they would also accept *Elizabeth II (by the Grace of God) of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, [name of realm], and all of her other Realms and Territories Queen, Head of the Commonwealth (Defender of the Faith)*.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETwomey2006106–107-37)[[35]](#cite_note-38) The latter composition was adopted, despite some objections from the South African and Canadian governments. The sovereign's title in all her realms thus kept mention of the United Kingdom, but, for the first time, also separately mentioned Australia and the other Commonwealth realms. The passage of the [Royal Style and Titles Act 1953](/wiki/Royal_Style_and_Titles_Act_1953 "Royal Style and Titles Act 1953") by the Parliament of Australia put these recommendations into law.[[36]](#cite_note-Titles_Act_1953-39)

the [Whitlam government](/wiki/Whitlam_government "Whitlam government") replaced this with the [Royal Style and Titles Act 1973](/wiki/Royal_Style_and_Titles_Act_1973 "Royal Style and Titles Act 1973"), with Whitlam arguing that the inclusion and position of Elizabeth's title in the UK made the title not "sufficiently distinctively Australian" and that the phrase "Defender of the Faith" had "no historical or constitutional relevance in Australia".[[37]](#cite_note-40) A new Royal Titles and Styles Bill that removed these references was passed by the federal Parliament.[[d]](#cite_note-44) The governor-general, [Sir Paul Hasluck](/wiki/Paul_Hasluck "Paul Hasluck"), reserved [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") for the monarch, as governor-general Sir [William McKell](/wiki/William_McKell "William McKell") had done with the 1953 Royal Titles and Styles Bill to allow Elizabeth to give her assent in person, which she did at Government House in Canberra on 19 October 1973.[[41]](#cite_note-Titles_Act_1973-45)

At the state level, Western Australia and South Australia have independently legislated the monarch's title to be the same as the Commonwealth title.[[42]](#cite_note-46)[[43]](#cite_note-47) There is limited reference to the monarch's title in the other states, however parliamentarians have used expressions such as "Queen of [state]" and "Queen in right of [state]" during parliamentary debates.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETwomey2011453–5,_475–6-48) In 1973, Queensland sought to pass legislation to include Queensland specifically in the monarch's title.[[45]](#cite_note-49) The Queensland parliament passed legislation seeking an advisory opinion from the [Privy Council](/wiki/Judicial_Committee_of_the_Privy_Council "Judicial Committee of the Privy Council") as to whether they had the legislative power to do, however this legislation was declared unconstitutional by the High Court.[[46]](#cite_note-50) In seeking this title, the Queensland government's motivation was to dissuade the British from accepting the Whitlam government's advice that all of the British government's then authority over the states should be transferred to the federal government. [The dismissal of the Whitlam government](/wiki/The_dismissal_of_the_Whitlam_Government "The dismissal of the Whitlam Government") in 1975 removed the impetus for the title change and no further steps were taken in the matter.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETwomey2006146–60-51)

### Succession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Succession")]

Further information: [Succession to the British throne](/wiki/Succession_to_the_British_throne "Succession to the British throne")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Queensland_State_Archives_4856_Proclamation_Ceremony_Parliament_House_c_1952_%28cropped%29.png/250px-Queensland_State_Archives_4856_Proclamation_Ceremony_Parliament_House_c_1952_%28cropped%29.png)](/wiki/File:Queensland_State_Archives_4856_Proclamation_Ceremony_Parliament_House_c_1952_(cropped).png)

The [proclamation of the accession of Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Proclamation_of_accession_of_Elizabeth_II "Proclamation of accession of Elizabeth II") to the Australian throne being read at [Queensland's Government House](/wiki/Government_House,_Brisbane "Government House, Brisbane") by [Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Queensland "Governor of Queensland") Sir [John Lavarack](/wiki/John_Lavarack "John Lavarack"), 1952

Royal succession is determined by a mix of common law, British law that continues to apply in Australia, and more recent Australian federal and state statutes. These entail that succession follows the eldest non-adopted child of the current monarch ([primogeniture](/wiki/Primogeniture "Primogeniture")), with the restriction that an heir must be in communion with the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") and not a [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") to ascend the throne.

#### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

These rules have evolved over centuries. The British statutes, the *[Bill of Rights 1689](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689")* and the *[Act of Settlement 1701](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement_1701 "Act of Settlement 1701")* first limited succession to legitimate descendants of [Sophia, Electress of Hanover](/wiki/Sophia_of_Hanover "Sophia of Hanover") and imposed religious requirements in the context of the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution"). These laws were received alongside all other British laws to Australia when Australia was settled. Considering the colonial status of the individual colonies and later the federated Australia, it was accepted at the time that these laws could only be changed by the UK Parliament.[[48]](#cite_note-:6-52)

Later, Australia and the other dominions gained greater legislative independence with the passage of the [Statute of Westminster 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931") ([adopted by Australia in 1942](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_Adoption_Act_1942 "Statute of Westminster Adoption Act 1942")).[[49]](#cite_note-sowaa1942-53) While this allowed the dominions to pass laws that conflicted with UK laws, to ensure that succession laws remained consistent, the preamble noted that it would be in keeping with each Commonwealth realm's constitutional practice that any succession changes would require the consent of the parliaments of each realm.[[49]](#cite_note-sowaa1942-53)[[48]](#cite_note-:6-52)

As Australia had not yet adopted the *Statute of Westminster* by the time of the [abdication of Edward VIII](/wiki/Abdication_of_Edward_VIII "Abdication of Edward VIII") in 1936, the UK *Declaration of Abdication Act 1936* applied automatically without the need for Australia's consent. However, the Australian federal Parliament did pass a resolution of assent to the changes as a matter of courtesy.[[50]](#cite_note-54)

The most recent reforms to the succession occurred following the [Perth Agreement](/wiki/Perth_Agreement "Perth Agreement") in 2011, in which all the Commonwealth realms agreed to changes including the removal of a preference towards male heirs and the repeal of the *[Royal Marriages Act 1772](/wiki/Royal_Marriages_Act_1772 "Royal Marriages Act 1772")* (which amongst other things prevented the monarch from marrying a Roman Catholic). As the Australian federal Parliament does not have a head of power over succession, it required a referral legislation from each of the states.[[51]](#cite_note-55)[[52]](#cite_note-56) The [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory") also added its request and concurrence, although this was not constitutionally required.[[53]](#cite_note-57) The federal legislation finally become law on 24 March 2015[[54]](#cite_note-58)[[55]](#cite_note-59) and as Australia was the last realm to the make the required changes, the act took effect on 26 March 2015 ([BST](/wiki/British_Summer_Time "British Summer Time")), parallel to other realms' laws.[[56]](#cite_note-60)

#### Demise of the Crown

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Demise of the Crown")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/2022_NSW_Proclamation_Ceremony.jpg/250px-2022_NSW_Proclamation_Ceremony.jpg)](/wiki/File:2022_NSW_Proclamation_Ceremony.jpg)

Crowds line the streets during the [proclamation ceremony for King Charles III in New South Wales](/wiki/Proclamation_of_accession_of_Charles_III#New_South_Wales "Proclamation of accession of Charles III"), 2022

Upon a [demise of the Crown](/wiki/Demise_of_the_Crown "Demise of the Crown") (the death or [abdication](/wiki/Abdication "Abdication") of a sovereign), it is customary for the accession of the new monarch to be publicly [proclaimed](/wiki/Proclamation "Proclamation") by the governor-general on behalf of the [Federal Executive Council](/wiki/Federal_Executive_Council_(Australia) "Federal Executive Council (Australia)"), which meets at Government House after the accession.[[57]](#cite_note-61) Parallel proclamations are made by the governors in each state.[[58]](#cite_note-62)[[59]](#cite_note-63) Regardless of any proclamations, the late sovereign's heir immediately succeeds, without any need for confirmation or further ceremony. Following an appropriate period of [mourning](/wiki/Mourning "Mourning"), the monarch is also [crowned](/wiki/Coronation_of_the_British_monarch "Coronation of the British monarch") at a coronation ceremony in the United Kingdom; though, this is not necessary for a sovereign to reign, being primarily a symbolic event.[[60]](#cite_note-64) For example, [Edward VIII](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII") was never crowned, yet was undoubtedly king during his short time on the throne. After an individual ascends the throne, he or she typically continues to reign until death.

The monarch legally cannot unilaterally [abdicate](/wiki/Abdicate "Abdicate"); the only Australian monarch to do so, [Edward VIII, did so](/wiki/Abdication_of_Edward_VIII "Abdication of Edward VIII") following the passage of British legislation. While the UK has passed [regency acts](/wiki/Regency_Acts "Regency Acts") from 1936 onwards to prepare for a situation when the monarch is incapacitated, the dominions did not agree for these acts to be extended into domestic law as it was felt that governors-general could exercise all the powers a regent would need to exercise.[[61]](#cite_note-:3-65) Issues could arise if the monarch was incapacitated for a particularly long period, as there is no other legal method for the governor-general to be replaced.[[62]](#cite_note-66)

### Finances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Finances")]

Australia does not fund the King or wider royal family for any activities taken outside of Australia, either towards personal income or to support royal residences outside of Australia. When monarch visits Australia, their expenses are paid for by the Australian Government.[[63]](#cite_note-67) However, the Australian Government does pay a salary to the governor-general and for the upkeep of the official vice-regal residences in the country.[[64]](#cite_note-68)

In 2018, a day-long visit to [Vanuatu](/wiki/Vanuatu "Vanuatu") by [Charles](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") (then the Prince of Wales), escorted by [Australian Minister for Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Minister_for_Foreign_Affairs_(Australia) "Minister for Foreign Affairs (Australia)"), [Julie Bishop](/wiki/Julie_Bishop "Julie Bishop"), in between a tour of Queensland and the Northern Territory, was paid for by the Australian government.[[65]](#cite_note-69) Charles III's [2024 Australian royal tour](/wiki/2024_royal_tour_of_Australia "2024 royal tour of Australia") cost $640,000, Elizabeth II's [2011 royal tour](/wiki/Royal_tours_of_Australia#2011 "Royal tours of Australia") $2,690,000, her [2006 tour](/wiki/Royal_tours_of_Australia#2000s "Royal tours of Australia") $1,450,000, her 1964 tour £179,000 and her first tour in 1954 cost £510,000.[[66]](#cite_note-70)[[67]](#cite_note-71)

### Residences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Residences")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Prince_William%2C_Catherine%2C_Prince_George_and_Peter_Cosgrove.jpg/250px-Prince_William%2C_Catherine%2C_Prince_George_and_Peter_Cosgrove.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prince_William,_Catherine,_Prince_George_and_Peter_Cosgrove.jpg)

The Duke and Duchess of Cambridge and Prince George of Cambridge at a reception hosted by Governor-General [Peter Cosgrove](/wiki/Peter_Cosgrove "Peter Cosgrove") at [Admiralty House, Sydney](/wiki/Admiralty_House,_Sydney "Admiralty House, Sydney"), 2014

The governor-general has two official residences: [Government House](/wiki/Government_House,_Canberra "Government House, Canberra") in [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra") (commonly known as "Yarralumla") and [Admiralty House](/wiki/Admiralty_House,_Sydney "Admiralty House, Sydney") in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney").

When [HMY *Britannia*](/wiki/HMY_Britannia "HMY Britannia") was in Australian waters and in use by the monarch of Australia, it was not available to British officials for meetings or promotions.[[68]](#cite_note-Mason179-72)

## Personification of the state

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Personification of the state")]

Further information: [The Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown")

The monarch is the locus of many [oaths of allegiance](/wiki/Oath_of_Allegiance_(Australia) "Oath of Allegiance (Australia)"). Various employees of the Crown are required by law to recite this oath before taking their posts, such as all members of the [Commonwealth Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Australia "Parliament of Australia") and of the state and territory parliaments, as well as most magistrates, judges, police officers, and justices of the peace. This is in reciprocation to the sovereign's coronation oath, taken most recently by Charles III who promised "to govern the Peoples of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland [and] your other Realms ... according to their respective laws and customs".[[69]](#cite_note-73)[[70]](#cite_note-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Queensland_State_Archives_4861_Mr_Macrossan_administrating_oath_to_Governor_Lavarack_at_the_Swearing_in_ceremony_c_1952.png/250px-Queensland_State_Archives_4861_Mr_Macrossan_administrating_oath_to_Governor_Lavarack_at_the_Swearing_in_ceremony_c_1952.png)](/wiki/File:Queensland_State_Archives_4861_Mr_Macrossan_administrating_oath_to_Governor_Lavarack_at_the_Swearing_in_ceremony_c_1952.png)

[John Lavarack](/wiki/John_Lavarack "John Lavarack") taking the [Oath of Allegiance](/wiki/Oath_of_Allegiance_(Australia) "Oath of Allegiance (Australia)") as the [governor of Queensland](/wiki/Governor_of_Queensland "Governor of Queensland") after Elizabeth II's accession to the throne, 1952

The prime minister, ministers and parliamentary secretaries also [make an oath or affirmation of office on their appointment](/wiki/Oath_of_Allegiance_(Australia)#Prime_minister,_ministers_and_parliamentary_secretaries "Oath of Allegiance (Australia)") to a particular ministry, which traditionally included a promise of allegiance to the monarch.[[71]](#cite_note-:0-75) However, the wording of this oath or affirmation is not written into law and beginning with swearing in of [Paul Keating](/wiki/Paul_Keating "Paul Keating"), all [Labor](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party") prime ministers have dropped the reference to the sovereign.[[72]](#cite_note-76)[[73]](#cite_note-77)[[71]](#cite_note-:0-75)

The [oath of citizenship](/wiki/Oath_of_citizenship#Australia "Oath of citizenship") similarly contained a statement of allegiance to the reigning monarch until 1994, when a pledge of allegiance to Australia and its [values](/wiki/Value_(ethics_and_social_sciences) "Value (ethics and social sciences)") was introduced. However the concept of allegiance to the monarch remains important constitutionally, especially in the context of determining whether a person is an "alien" for the purposes of [section 51](/wiki/Section_51_of_the_Constitution_of_Australia "Section 51 of the Constitution of Australia")(xix).[[74]](#cite_note-78)[[75]](#cite_note-79)[[76]](#cite_note-80)

### Head of state

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Head of state")]

See also: [Australian head of state dispute](/wiki/Australian_head_of_state_dispute "Australian head of state dispute")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Queen_Elizabeth_II_and_Michael_Jeffery%2C_2007.jpg/250px-Queen_Elizabeth_II_and_Michael_Jeffery%2C_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Elizabeth_II_and_Michael_Jeffery,_2007.jpg)

Queen Elizabeth II and Governor-General [Michael Jeffery](/wiki/Michael_Jeffery_(Australian_Army_officer) "Michael Jeffery (Australian Army officer)") at [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace")

Key features of Australia's system of government include its basis on a combination of written and unwritten rules, comprising the sovereign, [governors](/wiki/Governors_of_the_Australian_states "Governors of the Australian states"), and [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Australia "Governor-General of Australia").[[77]](#cite_note-Australia_2013._p.12-81) The [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia") does not mention the term "head of state".{{efn|*[Constitution](https://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/cth/consol_act/coaca430/)* (Cth) sections 2 to 4 and further sections refer to "the Queen" (at the time, [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria")) and [covering clause 2](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia#Covering_clauses "Constitution of Australia") requires that to be interpreted as referring to her "heirs and successors in the sovereignty of the United Kingdom"}.} According to the Parliament of Australia website, Australia's head of state is the monarch and its [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government#Parliamentary_heads_of_government "Head of government") is the prime minister, with powers limited by both law and convention for government to be carried on democratically.[[78]](#cite_note-82) However, the governor-general's website states that the office holder is in practice Australia's head of state.[[79]](#cite_note-83) A leading textbook on Australian constitutional law formulates the position thus: "The Queen, as represented in Australia by the governor-general, is Australia's head of state."[[80]](#cite_note-Blackshield-84)

Additionally, Queensland[[81]](#cite_note-85) and South Australia[[82]](#cite_note-86) describe the monarch as the head of state for their particular state. New South Wales[[83]](#cite_note-87) and Western Australia[[84]](#cite_note-88) on the other hand describe their governors as their respective heads of state, whilst Tasmania[[85]](#cite_note-89) and Victoria[[86]](#cite_note-90) state that the governor "exercises the constitutional power" of the head of state.[[87]](#cite_note-91)

While current official sources use the description *head of state* for the monarch, in the lead up to the [referendum on Australia becoming a republic](/wiki/1999_Australian_republic_referendum "1999 Australian republic referendum") in 1999, [Sir David Smith](/wiki/David_Smith_(public_servant) "David Smith (public servant)") proposed an alternative explanation that the governor-general is head of state. This view has some support within the group [Australians for Constitutional Monarchy](/wiki/Australians_for_Constitutional_Monarchy "Australians for Constitutional Monarchy").[[88]](#cite_note-92)

## Constitutional role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Constitutional role")]

Australia has a [written constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia") based on the [Westminster model](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") of government, implementing a [federal system](/wiki/Federal_system "Federal system") and a distinct [separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers"). It gives Australia a [parliamentary system of government](/wiki/Parliamentary_system_of_government "Parliamentary system of government"), wherein the role of the sovereign and governor-general is both legal and practical. The sovereign of Australia is represented in the federal sphere by the governor-general—appointed by the monarch on the advice of the [prime minister of Australia](/wiki/Prime_minister_of_Australia "Prime minister of Australia")—and in each state by a [governor](/wiki/Governors_of_the_Australian_states "Governors of the Australian states")—appointed by the monarch upon the advice of the relevant state premier.

### Executive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Executive")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Kevin_Rudd_being_sworn_in_as_Prime_Minister_on_27_June_2013.jpg/250px-Kevin_Rudd_being_sworn_in_as_Prime_Minister_on_27_June_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kevin_Rudd_being_sworn_in_as_Prime_Minister_on_27_June_2013.jpg)

[Kevin Rudd](/wiki/Kevin_Rudd "Kevin Rudd") being sworn in as Prime Minister of Australia by Governor-General [Quentin Bryce](/wiki/Quentin_Bryce "Quentin Bryce") on 27 June 2013

Section 61 of the Constitution vests the [executive power](/wiki/Executive_power "Executive power") of the Commonwealth in the monarch, to be exercisable by the governor-general. The governor-general is appointed by the monarch on the advice of the prime minister—the only role that the monarch must perform personally.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-11) The governor-general in turn (usually following elections) appoints the individual with or most likely to obtain the confidence of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/Australian_House_of_Representatives "Australian House of Representatives") to be prime minister.[[89]](#cite_note-GG-93) Other ministers are appointed by the governor-general on the advice of the prime minister, a subset of which form the [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Australia "Cabinet of Australia"). These ministers then advise the governor-general on the exercise of executive power, either directly or through the [Federal Executive Council](/wiki/Federal_Executive_Council_(Australia) "Federal Executive Council (Australia)"). Executive authority extends to functions falling under the [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative"), including the power to declare war and enter into treaties.[[90]](#cite_note-94)

As the monarch and the governor-general must in general act on advice, their roles are primarily symbolic and cultural, acting as a symbol of the legal authority under which all governments and agencies operate. For example, members of various executive agencies and judges are formally appointed by the governor-general, not the government of the day.

However, the governor-general is also understood to possess certain 'reserve powers', which are powers that they may exercise in the absence of or contrary to advice. These powers are not codified, but are a matter of constitutional interpretation and constitutional convention; consequently their identities are controversial.[[91]](#cite_note-:53-95)[[92]](#cite_note-:33-96)[[93]](#cite_note-97)

One of the reserve powers is to appoint—and, by implication, to dismiss—a prime minister. (The office of prime minister is not provided in the Constitution but exists by convention.) Its most prominent use was during the [1975 constitutional crisis](/wiki/1975_Australian_constitutional_crisis "1975 Australian constitutional crisis") when governor-general Sir [John Kerr](/wiki/John_Kerr_(governor-general) "John Kerr (governor-general)") dismissed the government of [Gough Whitlam](/wiki/Gough_Whitlam "Gough Whitlam") on the basis that his government had failed to secure the passage of supply.[[94]](#cite_note-98) During the crisis there was a concern about a "race to the palace", where the governor-general might have tried to dismiss the prime minister before the prime minister sought to advise the monarch to dismiss the governor-general. Following the dismissal, Whitlam and the speaker of the house each separately contacted the palace to reverse the governor-general's actions, but the palace replied that only the governor-general, and not the monarch, had power to appoint or dismiss a prime minister.

The monarch has the power to create [Australian honours](/wiki/Australian_honours_and_awards_system "Australian honours and awards system"); this is done by means of [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") issued on the recommendation of the prime minister.[[95]](#cite_note-99)[[96]](#cite_note-100)

### Parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Parliament")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Platinum_Jubilee-_Parliament_House.jpg/250px-Platinum_Jubilee-_Parliament_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:Platinum_Jubilee-_Parliament_House.jpg)

[Capital Hill](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Canberra "Parliament House, Canberra") illuminated in purple to mark [Elizabeth II's Platinum Jubilee](/wiki/Platinum_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II#Australia "Platinum Jubilee of Elizabeth II"), 2022. The meeting place for the [Parliament of Australia](/wiki/Parliament_of_Australia "Parliament of Australia") was opened by the Queen in 1988.

[Parliament](/wiki/Parliament "Parliament") consists of the sovereign, the [Senate](/wiki/Australian_Senate "Australian Senate") and the [House of Representatives](/wiki/Australian_House_of_Representatives "Australian House of Representatives").[[97]](#cite_note-101) The monarch's authority in the House of Representatives is represented by the [Mace of the House](/wiki/Ceremonial_mace#House_of_Representatives "Ceremonial mace"), which also represents the authority of the house itself and its [Speaker](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_Australian_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the Australian House of Representatives").[[98]](#cite_note-102) However, neither the sovereign nor the governor-general participate in the legislative process save for the granting of [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent"). Further, the constitution outlines that the governor-general alone is responsible for summoning, [proroguing](/wiki/Section_5_of_the_Constitution_of_Australia "Section 5 of the Constitution of Australia"), and [dissolving](/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament "Dissolution of parliament") the federal parliament.[[99]](#cite_note-103)

All federal, state and territorial legislation, except those in the [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory_Legislative_Assembly "Australian Capital Territory Legislative Assembly") (ACT), are enacted only with the granting of royal assent, done by the monarch, governor-general, relevant state governor, or [administrator](/wiki/Administrator_of_the_Northern_Territory "Administrator of the Northern Territory") in the case of the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory") (NT). This is done in the federal context by the governor-general signing two copies of the bill.[[100]](#cite_note-104)[[101]](#cite_note-105) If the law is one in which takes effect on [proclamation](/wiki/Proclamation "Proclamation"), the governor-general will also make this proclamation to which the [Great Seal of Australia](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_Australia "Great Seal of Australia") is then affixed in authentication of the corresponding [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent").[[102]](#cite_note-106)[[103]](#cite_note-107) The governor-general may reserve a bill *for the King's (or Queen's) pleasure*; that is, withhold their consent to the bill and present it to the sovereign for their personal decision. Under the constitution, the sovereign also has the power to disallow a bill within one year of the governor-general having granted royal assent.[[104]](#cite_note-108) The purpose of this section was originally to allow the UK government to supervise the workings of the Commonwealth parliament, as this power would only be exercised by the monarch as advised by their British ministers.[[105]](#cite_note-109) The power was never actually used and it is very unlikely that it will be used in the future.[[11]](#cite_note-:5-12) However, the monarch did on occasion refuse (or indicated they would refuse) assent to bills passed by state parliaments, the last time being in 1980 when British officials indicated they would advise the Queen to refuse assent for a New South Wales bill that attempted to unilaterally remove the involvement of British ministers in the appointment of the governor.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETwomey2006175–90-110)

### Courts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Courts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/King_Street_Court_House%2C_Sydney_01.jpg/250px-King_Street_Court_House%2C_Sydney_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Street_Court_House,_Sydney_01.jpg)

A judge's bench in a courtroom in Sydney, with a relief of the [royal arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_the_United_Kingdom "Coat of arms of the United Kingdom") positioned above

In [British constitutional law](/wiki/British_constitutional_law "British constitutional law"), the monarch is traditionally known as the *fount of justice*.[[107]](#cite_note-ACM-111)[[108]](#cite_note-112) However, federal judicial power is vested by the [Australian Constitution](/wiki/Australian_Constitution "Australian Constitution") directly to the High Court and other federal courts; with the Crown not involved with the exercise of this power.[[109]](#cite_note-113)[[110]](#cite_note-114) However, the Crown does participate in the judicial system, with criminal offences in most jurisdictions deemed to be offences against the sovereign. Proceedings for [indictable](/wiki/Indictment "Indictment") offences are brought in the sovereign's name in the form of *The King [or Queen] against [Name]* (typically shortened to *R v [Name]* standing for *Rex* for King or *Regina* for Queen).[[111]](#cite_note-115) However, offences in Western Australia and Tasmania are brought in the name of the particular state.[[112]](#cite_note-116)[[113]](#cite_note-117) Civil lawsuits against the Crown in its public capacity (that is, lawsuits against the government) are permitted due to statute.[[114]](#cite_note-118)[[115]](#cite_note-119) In international cases, as a [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") and under established principles of [international law](/wiki/International_law "International law"), the monarch of Australia is not subject to suit in foreign courts without the monarch's express consent. The *[prerogative of mercy](/wiki/Pardon "Pardon")* lies with the monarch, and is exercised in the state jurisdictions by the governors.[[116]](#cite_note-120)[[117]](#cite_note-121)

### States and territories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: States and territories")]

Further information: [Governors of the Australian states](/wiki/Governors_of_the_Australian_states "Governors of the Australian states")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Queen_Elizabeth_II_at_the_opening_of_NSW_Parliament_-_Royal_Visit%2C_1954_%2812108501385%29.jpg/250px-Queen_Elizabeth_II_at_the_opening_of_NSW_Parliament_-_Royal_Visit%2C_1954_%2812108501385%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Elizabeth_II_at_the_opening_of_NSW_Parliament_-_Royal_Visit,_1954_(12108501385).jpg)

Queen Elizabeth II and Prince Philip at the opening of the third session of the thirty-seventh [Parliament of New South Wales](/wiki/Parliament_of_New_South_Wales "Parliament of New South Wales"), 1954

In accordance with the *[Australia Act 1986](/wiki/Australia_Act_1986 "Australia Act 1986")*, the sovereign has the power to appoint, on advice tendered by the relevant [state premier](/wiki/Premiers_of_the_Australian_states "Premiers of the Australian states"), a [governor](/wiki/Governors_of_the_Australian_states "Governors of the Australian states") in each of the Australian states, who themselves appoint executive bodies, as well as people to fill [casual Senate vacancies](/wiki/Casual_vacancies_in_the_Australian_Parliament "Casual vacancies in the Australian Parliament"), if the relevant state parliament is not in session. The state governors continue to serve as the direct representatives of the monarch, in no way subordinate to the governor-general, and they carry out on his behalf all of the monarch's constitutional and ceremonial duties in respect of their respective state. The [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory") and the [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory") are constitutionally subordinate to the federal government, with an [administrator](/wiki/Administrator_of_the_Northern_Territory "Administrator of the Northern Territory") appointed by the governor-general on the advice of the [federal minister for Territories](/wiki/Minister_for_Regional_Development,_Local_Government_and_Territories "Minister for Regional Development, Local Government and Territories") taking the place of a state governor in the Northern Territory.[[118]](#cite_note-122) Like their governor counterparts, they largely are required to act on the advice of the [Northern Territory Government](/wiki/Northern_Territory_Government "Northern Territory Government"). The Australian Capital Territory has no equivalent position, with legislative and executive power devolved directly from the federal government to the [ACT Legislative Assembly](/wiki/ACT_Legislative_Assembly "ACT Legislative Assembly") and the [ACT Executive](/wiki/ACT_Government "ACT Government").

The monarch or the governor also forms part of [state parliaments](/wiki/Parliaments_of_the_Australian_states_and_territories "Parliaments of the Australian states and territories") as per respective [state constitutions](/wiki/State_constitutions_in_Australia "State constitutions in Australia"). However, in case of [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia"), neither the monarch nor the governor forms part of parliament, which only consists of two elected houses. However, bills passed by the [Parliament of South Australia](/wiki/Parliament_of_South_Australia "Parliament of South Australia") and all other state parliaments must be assented to by the governor in the name and on the behalf of the monarch.[[119]](#cite_note-stateparl-123)

All the powers and functions of the monarch in relation to the making of an act by a state parliament are exercisable only by the governor of that state. However, if the monarch is present in a state in person, he or she can exercise any of those powers and functions.[[119]](#cite_note-stateparl-123)

## Cultural role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Cultural role")]

### Royal presence and duties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Royal presence and duties")]

Further information: [Royal visits to Australia](/wiki/Royal_visits_to_Australia "Royal visits to Australia") and [List of Commonwealth visits made by Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/List_of_Commonwealth_visits_made_by_Queen_Elizabeth_II "List of Commonwealth visits made by Queen Elizabeth II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Investiture%2C_Royal_visit_of_Queen_Elizabeth_II_and_the_Duke_of_Edinburgh%2C_February_1963%2C_Government_House%2C_Sydney_-_photographer_Australian_Photographic_Agency_%287300133560%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Investiture,_Royal_visit_of_Queen_Elizabeth_II_and_the_Duke_of_Edinburgh,_February_1963,_Government_House,_Sydney_-_photographer_Australian_Photographic_Agency_(7300133560).jpg)

Elizabeth II knights an individual during her 1963 visit to Australia

The sovereign and their family have participated in events such as various centennials and bicentennials; [Australia Day](/wiki/Australia_Day "Australia Day"); the openings of Olympic and other games; award ceremonies; [D-Day](/wiki/Normandy_landings "Normandy landings") commemorations; anniversaries of the monarch's accession; and the like.

Other royals have participated in ceremonies organised by or involving Australia and other Commonwealth nations abroad, such as [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), then Prince Charles, at the 2015 [Anzac Day](/wiki/Anzac_Day "Anzac Day") ceremonies at [Gallipoli](/wiki/Gallipoli "Gallipoli"), the Duke of Edinburgh at the Anzac Day service in Hyde Park, London in 2024, or the Duke of Sussex's attendance at an [Australia House](/wiki/High_Commission_of_Australia,_London "High Commission of Australia, London") reception for Australian athletes in the 2014 [Invictus Games](/wiki/Invictus_Games "Invictus Games").[[120]](#cite_note-124)[[121]](#cite_note-125)[[122]](#cite_note-126)

Members of the royal family have also made private donations to Australian charities or causes, such as when Elizabeth II made a private donation to the [Australian Red Cross](/wiki/Australian_Red_Cross "Australian Red Cross") Appeal after the [Blue Mountains](/wiki/Blue_Mountains_(New_South_Wales) "Blue Mountains (New South Wales)") bushfires in 2009 and Prince William to the Australian Red Cross during floods in 2023.[[123]](#cite_note-royal.gov.uk-127)[[124]](#cite_note-128)

### The Crown and the Australian Defence Force

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: The Crown and the Australian Defence Force")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Aust-ww2-japan.jpg/250px-Aust-ww2-japan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aust-ww2-japan.jpg)

Governor-General [the Lord Gowrie](/wiki/Alexander_Hore-Ruthven,_1st_Earl_of_Gowrie "Alexander Hore-Ruthven, 1st Earl of Gowrie") signs a [declaration of war](/wiki/Declaration_of_war "Declaration of war") against [Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") as [John Curtin](/wiki/John_Curtin "John Curtin") looks on, 1941.

Section 68 of the [Australian Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia") says: "The command in chief of the naval and military forces of the Commonwealth is vested in the governor-general as the Queen's [monarch's] representative."[[125]](#cite_note-129) In practice, however, the governor-general does not play any part in the Australian Defence Force's command structure other than following the advice of the [Minister for Defence](/wiki/Minister_for_Defence_(Australia) "Minister for Defence (Australia)") in the normal form of executive government.[[126]](#cite_note-130) All personnel of the Australian Defence Force swear an [Oath of Allegiance](/wiki/Oath_of_Allegiance_(Australia) "Oath of Allegiance (Australia)") (or affirmation) to the Australian Monarch where they pledge to "resist his (or her) enemies."[[127]](#cite_note-131)

[Australian naval vessels](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Navy "Royal Australian Navy") bear the prefix *[His Majesty's Australian Ship](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Australian_Ship "His Majesty's Australian Ship")* (HMAS) and many organisational groupings of the defence force (such as the [Royal Australian Infantry Corps](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Infantry_Corps "Royal Australian Infantry Corps"), [Royal Australian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Air_Force "Royal Australian Air Force") and the [Royal Australian Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Engineers "Royal Australian Engineers")) carry the "royal" prefix.[[128]](#cite_note-132)

Members of the royal family have presided over military ceremonies, including Trooping the Colour ceremonies, inspections of the troops, and anniversaries of key battles. When Elizabeth II was in Canberra, she laid wreaths at the [Australian War Memorial](/wiki/Australian_War_Memorial "Australian War Memorial"). In 2003, Elizabeth acted in her capacity as Australian monarch when she dedicated the Australian War Memorial in [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_London "Hyde Park, London"), London.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monarchy_of_Australia#endnote_CNN1) In 2024, Charles III as part of his tour of Sydney and Canberra, laid wreaths at the Australian War Memorial, as well as presiding over a [Royal Fleet Review](/wiki/Fleet_review "Fleet review") of the Royal Australian Navy, in Sydney.[[129]](#cite_note-133)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/PrincessAnneRASigs1.jpg/250px-PrincessAnneRASigs1.jpg)](/wiki/File:PrincessAnneRASigs1.jpg)

[The Princess Royal](/wiki/Anne,_Princess_Royal "Anne, Princess Royal") inspects the [Royal Australian Corps of Signals](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Corps_of_Signals "Royal Australian Corps of Signals") as the unit's colonel-in-chief, 2000

Monarchs of Australia (or their spouse in the case of Elizabeth II)[[130]](#cite_note-134) are appointed to the highest ranks in each of the branches of the Defence Force. In 2024, Charles III was appointed to the Honorary Ranks of [admiral of the fleet of the Royal Australian Navy](/wiki/Admiral_of_the_fleet_(Australia) "Admiral of the fleet (Australia)"), [field marshal of the Australian Army](/wiki/Field_marshal_(Australia) "Field marshal (Australia)"), and [marshal of the Royal Australian Air Force](/wiki/Marshal_of_the_Royal_Australian_Air_Force "Marshal of the Royal Australian Air Force"), by the governor-general of Australia.[[131]](#cite_note-135) These ranks have always been held by members of the royal family, with the exceptions of field marshals Sir [William Birdwood](/wiki/William_Birdwood "William Birdwood") and Sir [Thomas Blamey](/wiki/Thomas_Blamey "Thomas Blamey").

Additionally, some members of the royal family are [Colonels-in-Chief](/wiki/Colonel-in-chief "Colonel-in-chief") of Australian regiments, including: the [Royal Regiment of Australian Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Artillery "Royal Australian Artillery"); [Royal Australian Army Medical Corps](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Army_Medical_Corps "Royal Australian Army Medical Corps"); the [Royal Australian Armoured Corps](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Armoured_Corps "Royal Australian Armoured Corps") and the [Royal Australian Corps of Signals](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Corps_of_Signals "Royal Australian Corps of Signals"), amongst many others.

### Australian royal symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Australian royal symbols")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Royal_Standard_of_Australia.svg/250px-Royal_Standard_of_Australia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Royal_Standard_of_Australia.svg)

The [King's Flag for Australia](/wiki/King%27s_Flag_for_Australia "King's Flag for Australia"), adopted in 2024

Royal symbols are the visual and auditory identifiers of the Australian monarchy. The main symbol of the monarchy is the sovereign. The monarch's portrait has appeared on all Australian coins, with King Charles III's portrait in use for newly minted coins since 2024.[[132]](#cite_note-136) The monarch's image also previously appeared on most Australian [definitive stamps](/wiki/Definitive_stamp "Definitive stamp") until 1973. Due to complaints by royalists about this change, the annual Queen's Birthday stamp was introduced in 1980.[[133]](#cite_note-137) The monarch has also appeared on the lowest denomination of all Australian banknotes created to-date, with Queen Elizabeth II's portrait currently appearing on the [five-dollar banknote](/wiki/Australian_five-dollar_note "Australian five-dollar note").[[134]](#cite_note-138)[[135]](#cite_note-139) However the [Reserve Bank](/wiki/Reserve_Bank_of_Australia "Reserve Bank of Australia") has announced that a new design that depicts Indigenous Australian culture and history instead of the new monarch will replace the existing design.[[136]](#cite_note-140)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Australian_%241_-_original_series_-_obverse.jpg/250px-Australian_%241_-_original_series_-_obverse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Australian_$1_-_original_series_-_obverse.jpg)

Australian one-dollar banknote, 1968, featuring a profile of Elizabeth II on the [obverse](/wiki/Obverse_and_reverse "Obverse and reverse"). The [one-dollar coin](/wiki/Australian_one-dollar_coin "Australian one-dollar coin") replaced this note in 1984.

A [crown](/wiki/St_Edward%27s_Crown "St Edward's Crown") is depicted on the Queensland and Victorian state badges (which are included on the [Australian coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Australia "Coat of arms of Australia"),[[137]](#cite_note-141)) and on [various medals and awards](/wiki/Australian_honours_and_awards_system "Australian honours and awards system").[[138]](#cite_note-142) For example, the crown's presence on the insignia of the Order of Australia represents the monarch's role as Head of the Order.[[139]](#cite_note-143) The sovereign is further both mentioned in and the subject of songs and loyal toasts.[[140]](#cite_note-144) Australia inherited the [royal anthem](/wiki/Honors_music "Honors music") "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")" (alternatively, "God Save the Queen" in the reign of a female monarch) from the United Kingdom. It was the national anthem of Australia until 1984, and has since been retained as the country's royal anthem, its use generally restricted to official occasions where the monarch or a member of the royal family is present.[[141]](#cite_note-145)[[142]](#cite_note-146)

His Majesty [The King's Flag for Australia](/wiki/King%27s_Flag_for_Australia "King's Flag for Australia"), approved on 30 August 2024 by Charles III, signifies his presence and is displayed when Charles is in Australia.[[143]](#cite_note-147) The flag was first used during the 2024 royal visit. The flag is used in the same way as the [Royal Standard of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Royal_Standard_of_the_United_Kingdom "Royal Standard of the United Kingdom"). Where practical, when it is flown on or outside a building, no other flag should be flown with it and is flown when the King is visiting Australia from all buildings, cars, boats or aircraft that he occupies. The flag features the six quarters of the Commonwealth Coat of Arms, surrounded by an ermine border which represents the federation.[[144]](#cite_note-148)

As in other Commonwealth realms, the [King's Official Birthday](/wiki/King%27s_Official_Birthday "King's Official Birthday") is a [public holiday](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Australia "Public holidays in Australia") and, in Australia, is observed on the second Monday in June in all [states and territories](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia"), except Queensland and Western Australia. In Queensland, it is celebrated on the first Monday in October, and in Western Australia it is usually the last Monday of September or the first Monday of October.[[145]](#cite_note-149) Celebrations are mainly official, including the Australian [Birthday Honours](/wiki/Birthday_Honours "Birthday Honours") list and military ceremonies.[[146]](#cite_note-150)[[147]](#cite_note-151)

Popular royal symbolism emerged during the reign of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria"), as a means of encouraging loyalty to the wider British Empire. The Crown symbolised the "imagined community of a global British people" with prime minister [Joseph Lyons](/wiki/Joseph_Lyons "Joseph Lyons") calling it "the great unifying element in the British Empire". Queen Elizabeth II stated in 1954 during her Australian tour that "the Crown is the human link between all the people who owe allegiance to me, an allegiance of mutual love and respect and never of compulsion". To Australia's Indigenous peoples, the Crown has symbolised the dispossession of their land and the violation of [their sovereignty](/wiki/Australian_Indigenous_sovereignty "Australian Indigenous sovereignty"), with the claiming of the east coat of Australia by James Cook done in the name of [King George III](/wiki/King_George_III "King George III"). However, the Crown has also symbolised protection of Indigenous rights, with several petitions made to the monarch by Indigenous leaders protesting rights violations.[[148]](#cite_note-152)[[149]](#cite_note-153)[[150]](#cite_note-154) The continued presence of royal symbols has been argued to act as a "social construction of reality", which reinforces their perception as a "positive role in national life". Such symbolism has been criticised as akin to [propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda "Propaganda"), that acts to counter arguments against the royals' social and political power.[[151]](#cite_note-155)

### Religious role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Religious role")]

Until its new constitution went into force in 1962, the [Anglican Church of Australia](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Australia "Anglican Church of Australia") was part of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"). Its titular head was consequently the monarch, in his or her capacity as [Supreme Governor of the Church of England](/wiki/Supreme_Governor_of_the_Church_of_England "Supreme Governor of the Church of England").[[152]](#cite_note-156) However, unlike in England, [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") was never established as a [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") in Australia.[[153]](#cite_note-157)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of monarchy in Australia](/wiki/History_of_monarchy_in_Australia "History of monarchy in Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Queen-Victoria-Statue-Outside-QV-Building.jpg/250px-Queen-Victoria-Statue-Outside-QV-Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen-Victoria-Statue-Outside-QV-Building.jpg)

[Statue of Queen Victoria](/wiki/Statue_of_Queen_Victoria,_Sydney "Statue of Queen Victoria, Sydney") in Sydney

The development of the Australian monarchy into the independent entity it is today began in 1770, when Captain [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook"), in the name of, and under instruction from, [King George III](/wiki/George_III "George III"), claimed the east coast of Australia.[[154]](#cite_note-158)[[155]](#cite_note-Buck-159) Colonies were eventually founded across the continent,[[156]](#cite_note-160)[[157]](#cite_note-161) all of them ruled by the [monarch of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom"), upon the advice of his or her British ministers, the [Secretary of State for the Colonies](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_the_Colonies "Secretary of State for the Colonies"), in particular. In 1901 the six colonies [united to form the Commonwealth of Australia](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia"), following the assent of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") to the *[Commonwealth of Australia Constitution Act](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia")*. However, this did not change the relationship of the monarch to the new nation, with her powers (such as the appoint of governors, governors-general and others set out in the Constitution) exercised in accordance with the advice of British ministers.[[16]](#cite_note-RAC-17)

This situation continued until after the [First World War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), where in response to calls from some Dominions for a re-evaluation of their status under the Crown after their sacrifice and performance in the conflict,[[80]](#cite_note-Blackshield-84): 110 the [Balfour Declaration of 1926](/wiki/Balfour_Declaration_of_1926 "Balfour Declaration of 1926") was issued following a series of [Imperial conferences](/wiki/Imperial_Conference "Imperial Conference"). The statement provided that the United Kingdom and the Dominions were to be "autonomous communities within the British Empire, equal in status, in no way subordinate to one another in any aspect of their domestic or external affairs, though united by a common allegiance to the Crown". The *[Royal and Parliamentary Titles Act 1927](/wiki/Royal_and_Parliamentary_Titles_Act_1927 "Royal and Parliamentary Titles Act 1927")*, an Act of the [Westminster Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"), was the first indication of a shift in the law, which ensured the independence of the office.[[158]](#cite_note-162) Another move to independence occurred in 1930, when the British government agreed that the Australian Cabinet would advise the sovereign directly on the choice of governor-general. The Crown was further separated by the *[Statute of Westminster 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931")*,[[159]](#cite_note-163) [adopted by Australia in 1942](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_Adoption_Act_1942 "Statute of Westminster Adoption Act 1942") (retroactive to 3 September 1939).[[160]](#cite_note-164)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Margaret_Joan_Spencer.jpg/250px-Margaret_Joan_Spencer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Margaret_Joan_Spencer.jpg)

[Prince Henry, Duke of Gloucester](/wiki/Prince_Henry,_Duke_of_Gloucester "Prince Henry, Duke of Gloucester") inspects the [Australian Women's Army Service](/wiki/Australian_Women%27s_Army_Service "Australian Women's Army Service") as the governor-general, 1945

The [Curtin Labor government](/wiki/Curtin_government "Curtin government") appointed [Prince Henry, Duke of Gloucester](/wiki/Prince_Henry,_Duke_of_Gloucester "Prince Henry, Duke of Gloucester"), as governor-general during the Second World War. Curtin hoped the appointment might influence the British to despatch men and equipment to the [Pacific War](/wiki/Pacific_War "Pacific War"), and the selection of the brother of [King George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") reaffirmed the important role of the Crown to the Australian nation at that time.[[161]](#cite_note-165) Queen Elizabeth II became the first reigning monarch to visit Australia in 1954, greeted by huge crowds across the nation. In 1967, Elizabeth's son, Charles III (then Prince Charles), attended school in [Geelong Grammar School](/wiki/Geelong_Grammar_School "Geelong Grammar School") in [Corio, Victoria](/wiki/Corio,_Victoria "Corio, Victoria").[[162]](#cite_note-166) Her grandson [Prince Harry](/wiki/Prince_Harry,_Duke_of_Sussex "Prince Harry, Duke of Sussex") undertook a portion of his gap-year living and working in Australia in 2003.[[163]](#cite_note-167)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Prince_Charles_visiting_Geelong_Grammar_School%2C_Corio%2C_Victoria%2C_Australia.jpg/250px-Prince_Charles_visiting_Geelong_Grammar_School%2C_Corio%2C_Victoria%2C_Australia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prince_Charles_visiting_Geelong_Grammar_School,_Corio,_Victoria,_Australia.jpg)

[Charles, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Charles,_Prince_of_Wales "Charles, Prince of Wales") with students of his Australian alma mater, [Geelong Grammar School](/wiki/Geelong_Grammar_School "Geelong Grammar School"), in 2006

The sovereign did not possess a title unique to Australia until the Australian Parliament enacted the *[Royal Styles and Titles Act](/wiki/Royal_Styles_and_Titles_Act "Royal Styles and Titles Act")* in 1953,[[36]](#cite_note-Titles_Act_1953-39) after the accession of Elizabeth to the throne, and giving her the title of *Queen of the United Kingdom, Australia and Her other Realms and Territories*. However, Elizabeth only acted as Queen of Australia at the federal level. At the state level, Elizabeth acted as Queen of the United Kingdom with state laws still subject to the *[Colonial Laws Validity Act](/wiki/Colonial_Laws_Validity_Act_1865 "Colonial Laws Validity Act 1865")*, which meant that UK laws overrode them when inconsistent. While the federal government adopted the *Statute of Westminster* in 1942, which removed the legislative restrictions of the *Colonial Laws Validity Act*, the states specifically asked to be excluded from the agreement. This was because they felt their interests were better protected whilst they were under the authority of the UK, which provided a buffer against the increasing power of the federal government.

Thus, the British government could still – at least in theory, if not with some difficulty in practice – legislate for the Australian states, and the governors in the states were appointed by and represented the sovereign of the United Kingdom, not that of Australia.[[164]](#cite_note-168) As late as 1976, the British ministry advised the monarch to refuse [Colin Hannah](/wiki/Colin_Hannah "Colin Hannah") another term as [Queensland's governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Queensland "Governor of Queensland"), after seriously considering unilaterally dismissing him due his breach of political impartiality, despite the recommendation of the then state [Bjelke-Petersen](/wiki/Joh_Bjelke-Petersen "Joh Bjelke-Petersen") government for his nomination.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETwomey200662–68-169)[[166]](#cite_note-170) Additionally, court cases from state supreme courts could be appealed directly to the [Judicial Committee of the Privy Council](/wiki/Judicial_Committee_of_the_Privy_Council "Judicial Committee of the Privy Council") in London, thereby bypassing the Australian High Court which otherwise could not be appealed in the privy council for federal matters since 1968 and for state matters since 1975.[[167]](#cite_note-171)[[168]](#cite_note-172) In 1973 reference to the United Kingdom was removed by the *Royal Style and Titles Act*. Henceforth, the monarch would be styled uniquely as *Queen of Australia*. Elizabeth signed her assent to the Act at Government House, Canberra that year, leading Senior Vice President of the Labor Party, [Jack Egerton](/wiki/Jack_Egerton "Jack Egerton"), to remark to her, "They tell me, love, you've been naturalised."[[169]](#cite_note-173)[[170]](#cite_note-174) It was with the passage of the *[Australia Act 1986](/wiki/Australia_Act_1986 "Australia Act 1986")*, which repealed the *Colonial Laws Validity Act* and abolished appeals of state cases to London, that the final vestiges of the British monarchy in Australia were removed, leaving a distinct Australian monarchy for the nation. The view in the [Republic Advisory Committee](/wiki/Republic_Advisory_Committee "Republic Advisory Committee")'s report in 1993 was that if, in 1901, Victoria, as Queen-Empress, symbolised the British Empire of which all Australians were subjects, all of the powers vested in the monarch under Australia's Constitution were now exercised on the advice of the Australian government.[[16]](#cite_note-RAC-17)

The [1999 Australian republic referendum](/wiki/1999_Australian_republic_referendum "1999 Australian republic referendum") was defeated by 54.4 per cent of the populace, despite polls showing that the majority supported becoming a republic.[[171]](#cite_note-175) Many commentators have argued that disagreement between republicans as to the preferred model for a republic (most notably over whether the president should be appointed or directly elected) was a key factor in the "no" result.[[172]](#cite_note-176)[[173]](#cite_note-177) The referendum followed the recommendation of a [1998 Constitutional Convention](/wiki/1998_Australian_Constitutional_Convention "1998 Australian Constitutional Convention") called to discuss the issue of Australia becoming a republic. The Queen visited Australia a year after the referendum and stated that "I respect and accept the outcome of the referendum. In the light of the result last November, I shall continue faithfully to serve as Queen of Australia under the Constitution to the very best of my ability".[[174]](#cite_note-178)[[175]](#cite_note-179)

Elizabeth II, the longest-serving monarch, [died on 8 September 2022](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Elizabeth_II "Death and state funeral of Elizabeth II"), and was succeeded by her son, Charles III. The [coronation of Charles III and Camilla](/wiki/Coronation_of_Charles_III_and_Camilla "Coronation of Charles III and Camilla") took place on 6 May 2023. In October 2024, Charles III became the first reigning King of Australia to [visit the country](/wiki/2024_royal_tour_of_Australia "2024 royal tour of Australia").[[176]](#cite_note-180)

## Debate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Debate")]

Further information: [Republicanism in Australia](/wiki/Republicanism_in_Australia "Republicanism in Australia") and [Monarchism in Australia](/wiki/Monarchism_in_Australia "Monarchism in Australia")

### Public polling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Public polling")]

Further information: [Republicanism in Australia § Public opinion](/wiki/Republicanism_in_Australia#Public_opinion "Republicanism in Australia")

Various polls have been conducted on public support for the monarchy since at least 1953. These have produced a variety of responses, depending on the specific framing of the question. A peer review analysis of survey data published in the *[Australian Journal of Political Science](/wiki/Australian_Journal_of_Political_Science "Australian Journal of Political Science")* in 1993 found that republican sentiment had slowly and stably increased from the 1950s and rose rapidly in the late 1980s and early 1990s such that certain polls indicated a majority or plurality republican support.[[177]](#cite_note-181) A follow up analysis published in 2016 found that support for the monarchy reached a low of 34 per cent in 1998 following the 1992 *[annus horribilis](/wiki/Annus_horribilis "Annus horribilis"),* before rising significantly to around 50% by 2016.[[178]](#cite_note-182)

Following the accession of King Charles in September 2022, the number of polls on the republic issue increased, [producing a range of responses](/wiki/Republicanism_in_Australia#Public_opinion "Republicanism in Australia").

### Political debate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Political debate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/2024_Royal_Visit_Sydney_%286%29.jpg/250px-2024_Royal_Visit_Sydney_%286%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2024_Royal_Visit_Sydney_(6).jpg)

Thousands of Australians greeting King Charles III at the Sydney Opera House, 2024

Whereas prime minister [Julia Gillard](/wiki/Julia_Gillard "Julia Gillard") stated that she would like to see Australia become a republic, she, on 21 October 2011, at a reception in the presence of the Queen at Parliament House, asserted that the monarch is "a vital constitutional part of Australian democracy and would only ever be welcomed as a beloved and respected friend".[[179]](#cite_note-183) After [Kevin Rudd](/wiki/Kevin_Rudd "Kevin Rudd") was appointed as prime minister, he affirmed that a republic was still a part of his party's platform and stated his belief that the debate on constitutional change should continue.[[180]](#cite_note-184)

Gillard had, during her time as prime minister, propounded that an appropriate time for Australia to become a republic would be after the end of the reign of Queen Elizabeth II. Following Elizabeth's death, the Prime Minister, [Anthony Albanese](/wiki/Anthony_Albanese "Anthony Albanese"), said in an interview he wanted Australia to have an appointed head of state, but he did not have a timetable for a referendum, did not commit to advising one take place during his time as prime minister, and postulated that no vote should happen until demand rose from the [grassroots](/wiki/Grassroots "Grassroots").[[181]](#cite_note-Morgan-185) Albanese had earlier stated he would, out of respect for Elizabeth, merely refrain from having the governor-general call a referendum before the next election for the House of Representatives.[[182]](#cite_note-186)[[183]](#cite_note-187)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Abolish_The_Monarchy_Melbourne_Naarm_Rally_%2852379052770%29.jpg/250px-Abolish_The_Monarchy_Melbourne_Naarm_Rally_%2852379052770%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abolish_The_Monarchy_Melbourne_Naarm_Rally_(52379052770).jpg)

A woman protesting the monarchy during the public day of mourning for Queen Elizabeth II, 2022

Republicans have dismissed the large public turnouts during royal tours as "the cult of celebrity".[[184]](#cite_note-188) However, following [Prince William](/wiki/Prince_William "Prince William")'s and [Catherine](/wiki/Catherine,_Princess_of_Wales "Catherine, Princess of Wales")'s visit to the Blue Mountains after devastating bush fires in 2014, historian [Jane Connors](/wiki/Jane_Connors_(academic) "Jane Connors (academic)") opined that "there is still a sense that having the royal family come to see you is more healing and significant than just having anyone come to see you", citing comments made by some who had come to the area while the royal couple were present.[[185]](#cite_note-189) Academic Holly Randell-Moon has criticised royal visits as reinforcing the legitimacy of [white settler possession](/wiki/Settler_colonialism "Settler colonialism") against [Indigenous sovereignty](/wiki/Australian_Indigenous_sovereignty "Australian Indigenous sovereignty"), with [celebrity](/wiki/Celebrity "Celebrity") responses to the royals "obscuring the racial and religious power" of monarchy.[[186]](#cite_note-190)

Former governor-general [Michael Jeffery](/wiki/Michael_Jeffery "Michael Jeffery") argued that the constitutional monarchy has been key to Australia's political stability and lack of civil wars.[[187]](#cite_note-191)

The idea of a uniquely Australian monarch, resident in Australia, has been voiced occasionally. The proposition was first published in 1867.[[188]](#cite_note-192) It was later reiterated by [Alan Atkinson](/wiki/Alan_Atkinson_(historian) "Alan Atkinson (historian)") in his 1993 book *The Muddle Headed Republic*,[[189]](#cite_note-193) by Harry Meklonian in 2009,[[190]](#cite_note-smh.com.au-194) and by Richard Hughes in 2017.[[191]](#cite_note-195) In a similar vein, [Waleed Aly](/wiki/Waleed_Aly "Waleed Aly") suggested in 2022 replacing the monarch with a life appointed [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") "First Elder".[[192]](#cite_note-196) Another possibility, offered in 2009 by an American constitutional lawyer, would be to crown someone in the line of succession to the Australian throne (such as [Prince Andrew](/wiki/Prince_Andrew "Prince Andrew") and [Prince Harry](/wiki/Prince_Harry "Prince Harry")), but who is not expected to become monarch by the present rules of succession.[[190]](#cite_note-smh.com.au-194)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Australia&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-accession_4-0)** Queen Elizabeth died at 3:10pm 8 September in Balmoral, UK which would have been 9 September in some Australian states.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The [Australian Government](/wiki/Australian_Government "Australian Government") acknowledges King Charles III's [accession day](/wiki/Accession_day "Accession day") as the day he came to the throne in the United Kingdom, 8 September.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
2. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-KingTitle_24-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-KingTitle_24-1) It has been noted that the inclusion of the word "King" before "Charles" in the King's title is inconsistent with that of Queen Elizabeth II and the King's title in other realms.[[22]](#cite_note-23)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-36)** All the states apart from Victoria proclaimed the King's title to be the same as that proclaimed at the federal level.[[26]](#cite_note-28)[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-31)[[30]](#cite_note-32) Victoria declared the monarch's title to be *His Majesty King* *Charles the Third, by the Grace of God King of Australia and His other Realms and Territories, Head of the Commonwealth*.[[31]](#cite_note-33) However, subsequent Victorian government documents have used the title *Charles the Third, by the Grace of God King of Australia and His other Realms and Territories, Head of the Commonwealth*.[[32]](#cite_note-34)[[33]](#cite_note-35)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-44)** A proposal to remove "the second" and "by the grace of God" was dropped after Elizabeth indicated her preference that those phrases remain.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETwomey2010451-41)[[39]](#cite_note-42)[[40]](#cite_note-43)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-197)** See [English claims to the French throne](/wiki/English_claims_to_the_French_throne "English claims to the French throne")
